subject: Statistics
title: Berkson's Paradox
description: Learn Berkson's Paradox in one minute.

hook.text: In hospital data, two unrelated diseases appear negatively correlated. Neither causes the other. What's happening?

buildup.text: Berkson's paradox occurs when you condition on a collider — a variable caused by both factors you're studying.

discovery.text: Hospital admission is the collider. Admitted for either disease, having one makes the other seem rarer.

twist.text: The dating 'attractiveness vs. niceness' trade-off may be Berkson's paradox. You filter on the sum.

climax.text: Conditioning on colliders creates spurious correlations. Causal diagrams help you spot them before they mislead.

punchline.text: Select on the sum and the parts look opposed. The filter creates the illusion.

quiz.question: What causes Berkson's paradox?
options: Conditioning on a collider variable that's caused by both factors, creating a spurious correlation
Having too few patients in the study
